posts.caption:
WEST’S SILENT OUTCRY
“It’s basically like the inside of my brain. It tells the story from when I was about 19 to now. It explores five different emotions, each just as intense as the others.
It’s called Man’s Ruin.” — Westislonely
Following the release of her new single ‘Bad Dog’ and ahead of a new EP due this July, Westislonely presents music rooted in the spirit of punk. Through striking black-and-white imagery, her music embodies the energy of rock, expressing her inner emotions with raw honesty.
The Westislonely full editorial will be available on our website soon.

posts.caption:
To celebrate the release of @oliviarodrigo’s new album “you seem pretty sad for a girl so in love,” @umusicshopcamden transforms into a special Rodrigo pop-up space. A2Z visited at opening time on opening day, where people were already queuing at the door. The pop-up is centred around the album’s theme colour, pink, and is filled with floral and grass installations. Visitors can shop limited-edition merchandise such as album-lettering T-shirts, hoodies, and eco bags, as well as an exclusive vinyl release, all of which reflect Rodrigo’s signature dreamy yet sweet sensibility. The pop-up runs until 13 June—don’t miss the chance to experience it.
📌Olivia Rodrigo Pop-up at UMusic Shop Camden
• Date: 12 (Fri) - 14(Sun) June
• Time: 12-13 June (10am-7pm), 14 June (12pm-7pm)
• Location: UMusic Shop Camden, Unit 832–835, The Stables Market, NW1 8AH
Words by Julie Jeong

posts.caption:
On 12 June, the influential British contemporary artist David Hockney passed away at the age of 88. Emerging from 1960s British Pop Art, Hockney began his career working across painting, printmaking, photography, stage design, and more recently, digital works presented at @serpentineuk, always searching for new ways to express what he saw. Over more than 60 years, he expanded the boundaries of art and left a deep influence on countless artists and audiences. Just as Hockney devoted his life to art, we will continue to cherish and remember his work for years to come.
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷Jean-Pierre Gonçalves de Lima
#DavidHockney #BritishArtist #PopArt

posts.caption:
Mint green is shaping up to be the colour of summer. From Chanel to Fendi, it appeared across runways in heels, handbags and dresses, proving itself one of the season’s most versatile colours. But it was Sarah Pidgeon’s description of her Chanel Métiers d’Art 2026 look as “toothpaste… but extremely chic toothpaste” that captured the mood best. Suddenly, mint was no longer just a pretty pastel. It became fashion’s freshest obsession.
And its appeal is not new. In the 1950s, mint appeared on cars, kitchens, interiors and fashion as a colour of postwar optimism and modern living. In the early 2010s, it returned through Tumblr moodboards, pastel nails, skinny jeans and iPhone cases. Now, it feels cooler, sharper and more grown-up.
Luxury houses are using mint to refresh their heritage codes, while younger generations are embracing it as a softer alternative to saturated colour. Beyond the runway, it is also appearing across makeup, watches, accessories and manicures.
Swipe to discover why fashion is falling for mint green again.
Words by Josephine Jiang
Next-Level Fashion, Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷@iicieststudio, @iramshelton, @charlotteroberts, @bea.kiune, @mac, @dualipa, @sideofsequins(Tiktok), Rear Window (1954), Vogue Runway,
#mintgreen #summercolor

posts.caption:
@keoghdewar’s methods are almost as unorthodox as their clothes. ⁠
⁠
To establish their visual language, the New Zealand-Scottish creative partners gathered 30 of their closest friends for a simple exercise. “We picked out the core garments that we wanted to begin with and asked them to wear them how they normally would,” says @alison_keogh, one half of the label. From there, they observed each person’s singularities and, more importantly, their shared habits.⁠
⁠
While their debut explored a pronounced tension between silhouette and materiality, the sophomore collection leans on colourful technical fabrics. Partly inspired by their relationship with stylist @harry_lambert, and the looks created for #HarryStyles’ ongoing tour, Keogh Dewar shift towards a more functional register. ⁠
⁠
At the link in bio, @pedromvasconcelos speaks with @alison_keogh and @katerosedewar about their new collection, Somewhere Else 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Keogh Dewar Spring/Summer 2027. Photography by @george_eyres_⁠
posts.alt_text: Photo by AnOther Magazine on June 12, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, makeup, magazine, poster and text that says 'AnOther 20 Questions with Kelsey Lu'.

posts.caption:
“Seven years of grieving” is how @iamkelseylu sums up the period since their debut LP, Blood. ⁠
⁠
Among these moments of personal reckoning, the North Carolina-born, Brooklyn-based cellist, singer, composer and performance artist has kept moving forward creatively, scoring acclaimed A24 drama Earth Mama and the Netflix documentary Daughters, collaborating with Jamie xx and Yves Tumor, and starring alongside Debbie Harry in Gucci’s 2025 Cruise campaign, shot by Nan Goldin. Now Lu is back with their second full-length project, So Help Me God, which arrives accompanied by a film co-directed with BAFTA-winner Savanah Leaf and starring Garance Marillier (Titane, Raw).⁠
⁠
At the link in bio, Lu talks @gemsamways through this ambitious second chapter and shares their thoughts on geology, vengeance and mentors.⁠
⁠
📸 Photography by @yumnaaa⁠
⁠
#KelseyLu

posts.caption:
Rest in peace David Hockney. The artist died peacefully at home at the age of 88.⁠
⁠
Hockney was perhaps Britain’s greatest living artist. The Bradford-born Royal Academician graduated from London’s Royal College of Art in 1962, his place in the British pop art scene already secured. To call him prolific would be an understatement. A pluralist long before the term became overused, he moved effortlessly between photo collage, video, multi-canvas landscapes, iPad drawings and opera set design, united by an extraordinary optimism and a singular way of seeing.⁠
⁠
His seductive colours and poised compositions have inspired generations of artists, filmmakers and designers, while holding centuries of art history within their frames. Through paintings, books and an irrepressible curiosity about how we experience the world, Hockney reminded us to look more closely, and with greater joy.⁠
⁠
To honour his work, revisit a digitised version of AnOther Magazine’s Art Project, taken from our Spring/Summer 2021 issue, which takes a new look at David Hockney’s work from 1966 to 1978. Link in bio 📲 ⁠
⁠
📸 #DavidHockney in 1965 © Cecil Beaton Studio Archive, Sotheby's London⁠

posts.caption:
It is a miracle that @paula_einfalt designs clothes at all. ⁠
⁠
In the 1990s, Einfalt’s parents founded the Croatian, then Yugoslavian, brand Skandal. It grew from near-nothing, into what Einfalt describes as an “empire” of 32 shops. Einfalt’s mother designed, her father managed the business, and together “they made millions” – until the 2007 economic crisis, that was. “They went bankrupt. We lost everything,” says Einfalt. “Then my dad got sick because of the stress and he died. I inherited a big part of his debt from the business.” ⁠
⁠
Now based in Paris, having spent a year working at McQueen, Einfalt has made her first collection outside of the scaffolding of CSM, photographed for AnOther by Alessandro Raimondo and styled by Rebecca Perlmutar. She is drawn to the 1920s and 1950s, to lace, old glamour and romantic clothing, but she does not want to make traditional lace dresses. “I want to bring it more into activewear,” she says. “Something really comfortable but still elegant and beautiful and empowering for women.” ⁠
⁠
Read more at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
Having studied painting in his hometown of Toronto, Canada, @matthewtammaro quickly turned to the immediacy of photography – but has never abandoned the shifting parameters of fine art. “It allows you to collage multiple ideas onto a two-dimensional surface in a way that relieves some of the frustration of photography.”⁠
⁠
Tammaro’s most recent venture, a photographic series and book titled Reconfigurations, was borne from this frustration and a wish to rediscover the playfulness of the camera. The project situates nude photography somewhere between eroticism and the grotesque. In the images, their naked bodies contort sometimes to the point of near-abstraction, sometimes to such intense focus that individual follicles and goosebumps are visible. “It’s based on the intimacy we have with our own and other bodies,” Tammaro tells @ella.slater. “We can be attracted to and repulsed by the same thing simultaneously.”⁠
⁠
See more at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Reconfigurations by Matthew Tammaro. Published by @oui_non_editions

Marlo Kelly is on the cusp of bona fide international stardom. The angel-faced Australian actor stars in the Emmy Award-nominated sci-fi series #3BodyProblem, and speaks to BAZAAR from Budapest, where she is preparing to start shooting season three. From a school production of ‘Macbeth’ and ‘Home and Away’ to her current role as “the scary lady from the show”, Kelly recounts the humble – and serendipitous – start of her acting career. “It feels like an idea that I was born with,” shares the self-described “theatre nerd”.
Through the link in bio, see @marlokelly photographed back home in Australia and continue reading the digital cover story.
Marlo Kelly wears @ralphlauren and @poloralphlauren on the June 2026 digital cover of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand.

“It is the luckiest thing that’s ever happened in my life,” says @marlokelly of the moment a talent agent signed her following her performance as Lady Macbeth in a school production. Upending the usual trope, the actor’s parents were supportive of her prioritising art over academics, on occasion. “One time [in year nine], I was worried about exams as well as a play,” she recalls. “And [my dad] was like, ‘don’t worry about your exams – just learn your lines.”
At the link in bio, continue reading BAZAAR’s interview with the #3BodyProblem star.

posts.caption:
Harper’s Bazaar India digital cover star, Maye Musk (@mayemusk), started modelling as a teenager. She stopped at 22 to get married and raise three children, then picked it back up at 28—an age the industry considered, at the time, well past the point of no return. So she built her nutrition practice in parallel. By 31, she was in high demand as a model and continued part-time; in her 60s, she was doing high fashion. “If someone had told me as a teenager that I would be in Delhi for a top fashion magazine at 78, I would have thought they were crazy,” she says. “Yet, here I am, working alongside a very creative team.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
In an industry where surnames are inherited currencies, Bazaar India’s April-May cover star, Tabu (@tabutiful), has operated for over three decades on a mononym. To understand her career is to understand what it meant to say yes to things the industry was not ready for and to have the instinct to recognise those things as worthy, even when no template existed for them. But she refuses to call it rebellion. “It was simply a reflection of who I am. The roles I chose, the decisions I made—they came from some similar place throughout my life. Some part of me has been completely unchanged,” she insists.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Founded by Mrs. Handa in 1992, Sarita Handa (@saritahanda) began by collaborating with women artisans who carried generations of Indian textile knowledge in their hands. Indian crafts such as hand quilting, patchwork and embroidery found recognition in international markets, becoming part of a larger design story shaped by skill, material and the maker.
From handmade beginnings to global manufacturing, every stage at Sarita Handa, from fibre to finish, is shaped by responsible sourcing, certified processes and careful making.
Today, through seven retail stores across India and a forthcoming iconic Delhi address, Sarita Handa continues to bring its craft-led world into contemporary Indian living.
As Sarita Handa completes 34 years, the journey continues through the Sarita Handa Archive (@saritahandaarchive). “For me, the Archive is both a record of the past and a source for the designs of tomorrow. It is built to preserve textile heritage, return to India’s craft histories and honour the skills we keep alive and the artisans who grow with the work,” she adds.
#BazaarIndia #SaritaHanda #MrsSaritaHanda #IndianCraft #TextileHeritage

posts.caption:
“My mayor Muslim / My bagel’s Jewish / My Christian Dior / Knicks in four!!!” A clean sweep of the NBA finals escaped the New York Knicks this week, but the instant virality of those four bars, bellowed into a camera by Queens-native MD Ahnaf Hossain after the team’s Game 1 win, captured the infectious fervour that has made Knicks tickets the hottest commodity in town.
While fewer people seem interested in paying $10,000 for a handbag these days, plenty were willing to pay that much or more to see — and be seen at — a Knicks game.
That kind of mania surely has lessons for a fashion industry aiming to reignite consumer interest after a prolonged downturn.
BoF’s Vikram Alexei Kansara (@vikramkansara) explores how Knicks fever can teach fashion a thing or two about the power of collective experiences, can’t-miss moments and organic celebrity endorsements. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @nyknicks, @nba, @kalshisports, Getty Images

posts.caption:
Since Camille Miceli joined Pucci in 2021, one message has been unmistakably clear: Pucci and its arsenal of swirling mid-century prints are a stand-in for fun in the sun.
The brand’s signature vibrant silk foulard is emphasised throughout each collection, from scarf tops and skirts to silk satchels and twillie-adorned T-shirts. These collections are staged once a year, avoiding the fashion calendar to ensure a dedicated media moment.
The brand’s ultra-focused approach continues to gain traction, with Miceli’s legible, unabashedly commercial celebration of Pucci’s prints and jet-set identity sparking a revival for the brand after years of dwindling sales had led owner LVMH to cut its business down to the bone.
In the latest High Margin newsletter, Robert Williams (@robert.williams.writes) examines how Pucci turned focus into growth. Read more and subscribe #linkinbio
📷 @emiliopucci

posts.caption:
After years of cautious Pride marketing, brands are ditching rainbow clichés for sexier, more subversive campaigns.
It’s a reflection of the zeitgeist at large. Even in just the past year, the LGBTQ+ community has gained a greater level of mainstream recognition. But trepidation around gay marketing hasn’t disappeared. Brands can face conservative backlash for championing the LGBTQ+ community and the generic rainbow motifs of Pride months past feel more like a performative gesture than an authentic effort to connect with the queer community.
What does Pride marketing look like after the rainbow logo era? BoF's Austin Kim (@austintaekim) investigates. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @levis, @boysmells, @away

posts.caption:
On Tuesday, The Business of Fashion and LTK convened senior leaders from @abercrombie, @harveynichols, @glossier, @rixo, @aligne, @theoutnet, @elfcosmetics, @needleandthreadlondon, @demellierlondon and more for a private dinner in London.
Ahead of dinner, BoF’s @AliceGividen sat down with LTK co-founder @AmberVenzBox for a fireside chat to discuss the new fundamentals of creator commerce and how brands are adapting to a rapidly changing discovery landscape.
The conversation explored the growing importance of trust, the need for more connected creator data and infrastructure, and how brands are navigating a future shaped by fragmented discovery, changing consumer behaviours and AI.
Over dinner, guests shared perspectives on what it takes to create meaningful connections with consumers today.

posts.caption:
The rise of GLP–1 drugs, such as Ozempic and Wegovy, is forcing fashion and beauty companies to rethink everything from sizing and fit to product development. With one in eight Americans having tried a GLP–1 medication, brands are grappling with how to serve consumers whose bodies may be changing more rapidly than traditional product cycles were designed to accommodate.
In this episode of The Debrief, senior correspondent Sheena Butler-Young (@sheena.butler.young) sits down with BoF senior news and features editor Diana Pearl (@dianaspearl) and The Business of Beauty news and features editor Brennan Kilbane (@brennankilbane) to discuss how fashion and beauty brands are responding to the GLP-1 boom — and why the industry’s apparent willingness to adapt to these consumers is raising difficult questions about its long history with size inclusivity.
🎙️ Listen now #linkinbio #TheDebriefPodcast
📷 Pexels

posts.caption:
After months of speculation surrounding their reported wedding, Isha Rikhi (@isharikhi) has finally shared a photo with husband Badshah (@badboyshah)—and fans couldn’t be more delighted. Earlier this year, wedding pictures of the couple surfaced online, sparking widespread buzz, but neither Isha nor Badshah publicly addressed the reports. Now, in a candid Instagram Story, Isha responded to a fan who asked why she had never posted a picture with her husband, sharing a sweet loved-up snapshot of the two together. The heartwarming photo shows the couple looking cosy and completely at ease in each other’s company. While they have largely kept their relationship out of the spotlight, this intimate moment offers a rare peek into their love story—and perhaps the soft confirmation fans had been waiting for.
📷: Isha Rikhi
#BridesTodayIn #BridesTodayIndia

posts.caption:
British painter David Hockney, whose vibrant portraits and sun-drenched depictions of the everyday made him one of contemporary art's most beloved figures, has died at 88.
The artist died "peacefully at home" on Thursday, one month short of his 89th birthday, according to a statement provided to CNN by his longtime publicist.
Some of his best-known works are a series of light-filled swimming pool paintings that seemed to freeze a moment in time. But his oeuvre was diverse, spanning photography, printmaking and stage design for ballet and opera productions.
In 2018, "Portrait of an Artist (Pool with Two Figures)" fetched $90.3 million to become (if only briefly) the most expensive work by a living artist ever to sell at auction.
Tap the link in bio for more.
📸 : Thomas Coex/AFP/Getty Images
posts.alt_text:
Elon Musk just became the world’s first trillionaire after SpaceX, his rocket and AI company, went public on Friday.

It’s a staggering amount of wealth never seen before in the history of human commerce. CNN’s Hadas Gold reports on the record-breaking IPO.

Follow live updates at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
It's the kind of celebrity snap you might scroll past on your phone several times a day – @davidbeckham and his pop singer girlfriend @victoriabeckham on an evening out. There's a sense of summer ease; both are tanned, dressed in casual black tops and trousers, sandals on.
Except the athlete is the world-famous David Beckham, and he's wearing a patterned sarong around his waist. In 1998 Britain, this was front page news.
"Beckham has got his Posh frock on," declared The Sun newspaper, alongside a full-page photo of the England player and Victoria "Posh Spice" Adams, as she was known at the time, in France for the World Cup.
This wasn't just any frock, mind you. This was a @jeanpaulgaultier wrap that challenged traditional notions of how a top male soccer player should appear, sending the British tabloids into a hysterical spin.
Tap the link in our bio to read more.
📸: Harry Page/The Sun/News Licensing/ Justin Goff Photos/Getty Images/David Abiaw/Shutterstock/Richard Sellers/Allstar/Getty Images

Starring Freja Beha Erichsen, Abbey Lee Kershaw, and Brad Kroenig, the campaign reflected Karl Lagerfeld’s vision of Chanel through a distinctly urban lens. Photographed by Lagerfeld himself and styled by Alex White, the images blended effortless cool with the house’s unmistakable elegance.

posts.caption:
A selection of looks that stayed on our minds this week.
Pulled from old paparazzi images, backstage moments, runway archives, and forgotten corners of the internet, these are the outfits we found ourselves returning to again and again.
Some lean into chaos, others into simplicity. Tiny knits, metallic textures, oversized sunglasses, low-slung tailoring, worn-in boots, layered jewelry, and makeup that looks better slightly undone. Different eras, different aesthetics, but all connected by a distinct sense of individuality.
What draws us in is their authenticity. Nothing feels forced, overly polished, or calculated. Many of these looks ignore conventional styling rules entirely—and that’s exactly what makes them memorable.
The outfits we couldn’t stop thinking about this week.
If you recognize yourself in any of these images, please send us a message so we can properly credit and tag you.

posts.caption:
Twenty years later, this editorial feels less like satire and more like reality.
Photographed by Steven Meisel for Vogue Italia in July 2005, „Makeover madness“ explored society’s growing obsession with youth, perfection, and cosmetic enhancement long before social media turned beauty into a 24-hour performance. What initially appeared shocking, a cast of supermodels wrapped in bandages, recovering from fictional procedures and surrounded by surgical instruments, was in fact a sharp critique of the pressures driving the pursuit of an “improved” self.
At the center of the story is Linda Evangelista, whose transformed appearance blurs the line between glamour and discomfort. Across nearly eighty pages, Meisel constructed a world where luxury hotels became recovery rooms and beauty itself became a form of consumption. The images are cinematic, unsettling, and deliberately excessive.
What makes Makeover Madness so powerful today is its relevance. The editorial questioned a culture increasingly fixated on correction, optimization, and eternal youth, a conversation that has only intensified in the age of filters, injectables, and algorithm-driven beauty standards. Rather than celebrating transformation, Meisel exposed the anxiety that often lies beneath it.
Nearly two decades later, the editorial remains one of fashion’s most memorable examples of social commentary: provocative, uncomfortable, and remarkably prophetic.
Editorial: Makeover Madness
Magazine: Vogue Italia, July 2005
Photographer: Steven Meisel
Fashion Editor/Stylist: Edward Enninful
Creative Director: Jason Duzansky
Hair: Guido Palau
Makeup: Pat McGrath
Set Design: Jack Flanagan
Models: Linda Evangelista, Elise Crombez, Eugenia Volodina, Missy Rayder, Julia Stegner, Inguna Butane, Hana Soukupova, Jessica Stam

posts.caption:
Why are these people sitting on the floor?
Because the floor is the speaker.
Circuits run beneath the surface. Sound doesn’t come from the walls — it moves through the ground, into the cushion, into your body.
You’re not listening to it. You’re inside it.
This is MPa Sound System: Discs.
Tokyo, CON Gallery.
At the center of the room, VQ sits on a disc generating live sound from an iPhone. That signal travels outward through radiating lines, connecting disc to disc, all the way to where the audience sits.
To be in this room is to be part of the circuit.
The instrument? A zipper jacket.
Zippers pulled back and forth not to open anything, but to hear what they do.
A gesture every child makes. A function no one designed it for.
This installation was made by PLASTIC PRODUCT.
A Korean brand but that label doesn’t quite fit.
They work with the fixed associations we attach to everyday objects. A coat hanger. A jacket zipper. A watch.
And they ask: what else could this be?
They don’t answer that question for you.
They place the object in front of you, and leave the rest.
@plastic_product isn’t asking you to consume faster.
They’re asking you to notice something you’ve stopped seeing.

posts.caption:
Ahead of the 2026 FIFA World Cup, multiple brands and fashion houses announced exciting partnerships, channeling the spirit of competition that will define this upcoming month.
Eager to capitalize on this football extravaganza, Jacquemus partnered with Nike to dress the French national team, while Loewe made a commitment to design the outfits for the Spanish players. Others, such as Balenciaga, Levi’s or Willy Chavarria (in collaboration with Adidas), released football-inspired collections, from elevated ready-to-wear to footwear and accessories.
As we can see, fashion and sports share a deep connection, and Yves Saint Laurent was well aware of it. Twenty-eight years ago, an hour before the 1998 World Cup finale kicked off, the Stade de France became a literal runway. As the French and Brazilian teams were about to offer us a historic game, the house’s savoir-faire filled the stadium, spreading across a YSL-trademarked lawn.
Indeed, on the 12th of July 1998, to celebrate his career’s 40th birthday, Yves Saint Laurent staged a monumental fashion show, displaying 300 of his most iconic silhouettes. Requiring the work of 900 people, the show was divided in 6 different themes such as pop art, les glamours or les orientaux, each including 50 models. The stadium became a pedestal for the designer’s incomparable legacy, unfolding under the eyes of 1.7 billion viewers.
For fifteen minutes, the 1990’s most celebrated models such as Katoucha, Carla Bruni, Laetitia Casta, Adriana Karembeu (then Sklenaříková), Noémie
Lenoir, Chrystèle Saint Louis Augustin and Imany took part in a thoroughly-crafted choreography, sporting the designer’s signature pieces. Ending on a topmodel-made YSL logo, the show sacralized the long-lasting history that sports and fashion always shared, while honoring the work of a one-of-a-kind designer.
While Yves Saint Laurent’s contribution to the 1998 World Cup brought undeniable luck to his country’s team, we shall see if this year’s collaborations can secure such success.
Written by Raphaël Vidal.
@raph_vdl

posts.caption:
Betsey Johnson’s Fall 2004 collection is a celebration of fashion as performance. Every look feels like a character stepping onto a stage that never stops moving.
There is a deliberate sense of chaos running through the collection: clashing prints, exaggerated silhouettes, playful layering, and an unapologetic use of color that rejects subtlety entirely. Instead of refinement, Johnson leans into exaggeration, turning each outfit into a statement of individuality and attitude.
What makes the collection compelling is not its adherence to traditional beauty standards, but its refusal to take them seriously. It exists in a space where fashion becomes costume, and costume becomes identity, fluid, loud, and self-aware.
FW2004 captures a moment where Betsey Johnson fully embraces her own universe: unapologetic femininity, ironic glamour, and a sense of humor embedded into every seam. It is not about dressing quietly, it is about being seen, fully and without compromise.

posts.caption:
David Hockney – the pioneering British artist whose vibrant paintings, swimming pools and landscapes redefined contemporary art – has passed away at the age of 88. 🕊️⁠
⁠
Hockney's death was announced today, with tributes already pouring in for one of the most influential and beloved artists of the 20th and 21st centuries. ⁠
⁠
Over a career spanning more than six decades, he became known for iconic works including A Bigger Splash and Portrait of an Artist (Pool with Two Figures), as well as his constant experimentation with photography, digital art and new technologies.⁠
⁠
The celebrated artist passed away peacefully at home on 11 June, just weeks before his 89th birthday.⁠
⁠
#DavidHockney #Dazed

posts.caption:
The future of natural diamonds won’t be won through supply alone.
It will be won through meaning.
ALROSA’s newly approved 2030 promotion strategy signals a major shift in focus — from production and pricing to storytelling, education, transparency and consumer confidence.
The question is no longer:
“Can the industry sell more diamonds?”
It’s:
“Can the industry communicate why they matter?”
💬 What will drive natural diamond demand the most over the next decade?
#DiamondWorld #NaturalDiamonds #ALROSA #DiamondIndustry #DiamondRetail
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by Diamond World on June 11, 2026 tagging @reenaahluwalia. May be an illustration of poster, magazine, calendar and text that says 'DW DIAMONDWORED ORED The Billion-Year Year Paradox Do We Ever Truly Own a Diamond? Canada-based visual artist Reena Ahluwalia poses the "Century Question": How does a diamond's legacy survive the hand that currently holds it?'.

posts.caption:
Diamonds are among the few objects on Earth that will outlast all of us. That raises a profound question:
Do we ever truly own a diamond?
In her compelling essay, Canada-based visual artist Reena Ahluwalia introduces the Billion-Year Paradox  a philosophical framework that redefines diamonds not as possessions, but as enduring witnesses to human stories.
Key Takeaways for the Jewellery Industry
💎 A natural diamond is a 3-billion-year geological miracle.
💎 Human ownership is temporary. Stewardship is enduring.
💎 In a world of deepfakes and digital noise, diamonds represent authenticity.
💎 Storytelling, provenance, and emotional relevance are becoming the true drivers of value.
💎 The brands that win will be those that connect fleeting moments to timeless meaning.
We never own diamonds. We only borrow their forever.
How does your brand communicate the enduring meaning of natural diamonds?
#NaturalDiamonds #DiamondWorld #JewelleryRetail #DWMagazine

posts.caption:
Let's think for a moment... Lab-grown diamonds were invented in the 1950s through HPHT by H. Tracy Hall. Later, diamond CVD emerged through the pioneering work of brilliant scientists such as Boris Derjaguin, William G. Eversole, Nobuo Setaka, Satoshi Matsumoto, Angus C. Wilson, John E. Butler, and many others.
More recently, generations of exceptional researchers have refined these technologies to the point where we can now even choose the colors of single-crystal diamonds, no longer depending on geological events, environmental destruction, or human exploitation to obtain gorgeous gemstones and develop new technologies based on diamond's extraordinary material properties.
To come across an Instagram post claiming that this is not history made is shameful, to say the least.

posts.caption:
Betsy Ross is turning in her grave.
American flag-inspired looks for the “Octagon Girls,” the official ring card models for the UFC, have been revealed for Sunday’s fight on the White House lawn.
“The inspiration was born from the intersection of sport, spectacle, and celebration,” costume designer Marina Toybina told Maxim. Toybina is a seven-time Emmy winner, winning three alone for her work on ‘The Masked Singer’...lol.
“Working closely with UFC, we wanted to create looks that honored both the energy of the brand and the significance of the 250th anniversary celebration.”
The sparkly looks include hot pants, bustiers, and multiple flag code violations. According to US Code Title 4 Chapter 1, “the flag should never be used as wearing apparel” and “no part of the flag should ever be used as a costume or athletic uniform.”
The images are unsurprisingly being dragged online. “Looks like a failed design challenge on Project Runway,” wrote one Threads user. “Tacky outfits for a tacky event being hosted by a tacky administration,” wrote another.
This weekend’s Freedom 250 fight will take place on Trump’s 80th birthday, which also happens to be Flag Day.
#flag #costume #news #threads #viral

posts.caption:
To promote her new album which comes out in July, Madonna released a short film called ‘Confessions II’ yesterday on YouTube. The film, directed by the creative production company ‘Torso’ which is also responsible for Charli XCX’s new SS26 video, premiered last week to an exclusive audience at Tribeca film festival.
Madonna recommends watching multiple times due to the dizzying amount of easter eggs and references included within. Fans are taking the hint; Already, Madonna lovers have dissected the ten-ish minute film, closely examining everything from celebrity cameos to a host of discreet audio samples from other hit songs.
One user notes ‘Danceterias’ use of a melody from Lou Reed’s ‘Walk on the Wild Side’, for example, while others note the use of the iconic beat from ‘Lucky Star.’ A Julia Garner cameo nods to the potential upcoming Madonna biopic which was shelved in 2023 and is now in Hollywood limbo. A scene with Sabrina Carpenter suggests Madonna’s proclivity for passing the popstar torch, as she did with Britney Spears at the VMA’s in 2003.
But the most jaw-dropping imagery in the film comes in the form of green laser beams shooting out of Madonna’s dancers’ orifices. Vaginal laser beams… What’s the reference there? Maybe there isn’t an easter egg in that scene, except to say that Madonna has always been a provocateur. She built a career on it, dancing in a wedding dress to ‘Like a Virgin’ and wearing Jean Paul Gaultier’s cone bra on tour in the 90s. Cone bras don’t shock audiences like they did thirty years ago, so perhaps the modern equivalent (if there is one) is vaginal/anal laser beams.
We won’t soon forget the image of these lasers, partly because of where they are emanating from, and partly because they demonstrate Madonna’s ability to reinvent herself. At 67 years old, she can still create startling new material, even when, in the case of ‘Confessions II’, she is re-visiting her own past. Bravo! And viva Madonna!

posts.caption:
Divyanshu Joshi, a 26-year old Delhi-based model, drowned while on location prior to an ad shoot for Kartik Research. He was also a former retail manager for the brand.
The tragedy occurred at Pettamala, a stone quarry that was shut down decades ago after multiple deaths. While the quarry is off limits, locals reportedly enter the area sometimes for bathing, washing clothes, or recreation. It is known for scenic beauty but also for dangerously deep pools which have been called ‘death traps’ because of low temperatures and an overgrowth of aquatic vegetation. In 2017, three students drowned in pools so deep that scuba divers sent to recover the bodies found the conditions challenging.
Kartik Research, the popular Delhi-based brand that was an LVMH semi-finalist in 2023, planned to do an ad shoot at Pettamala the following day, and was scouting the location when Divyanshu apparently entered the water, got out, and then went back in, slipping suddenly from the shallow edge into a deep chasm. Video taken of the incident shows the young model struggling to stay afloat. The brand had not acquired permission to shoot at Pettamala, and reports of the incident have varied, with some claiming that the brand’s founder dove in to rescue Divyanshu.
“As inaccurate accounts of this tragedy continue to circulate, we wish to clarify that Divyanshu was in Kerala as part of a seasonal photo shoot and was not participating in any swimming related activity as part of the production,” Kartik Research posted on their Instagram this week.
Many in the fashion industry are noting how the incident reflects their personal experiences on set. Safety is often overlooked on photoshoots, especially with smaller indie brands that are tasked with generating powerful creative despite budgeting and time constraints. How many fashion shoots take place everyday in impromptu or even illegal locations? How many times has a model neglected to advocate for their own health and safety (mental or physical) because they worried it would reflect poorly on them?
Divyanshu’s tragic and untimely death should be a wake up call to the industry.

posts.caption:
Selkie just released imagery for a new collection inspired by the American flag.
Fans of the brand are confused; How could Selkie—which has always been a vehicle for LGBTQ+ voices, plus-size advocacy, diversity, and women’s liberation—put out a patriotic line during a time when patriotism is impossible to untangle from fascism?
To Selkie, that was the intention.
“We refuse to surrender the symbols of this country to those who would use them against us,” reads the Instagram caption below a carousel of women in ruffled, patinated stars-and-stripes dresses.
That is, Selkie wants to reclaim the flag. In their posts, they cite how suffragettes reclaimed stars-and-stripes by sewing customized flags celebrating states where women could vote. This is a tough sell; The flag is difficult to ‘reclaim’ if it was historically wielded against you, as in the case of slaves uprooted from their ancestral countries or native tribes whose country was uprooted from beneath their feet.
There have been instances throughout fashion history when oppressive, colonialist flags were reclaimed for the sake of rebellion. The Union Jack, for example, was used by punks in the 70s to criticize the value systems of the establishment. Vivienne Westwood’s ‘God Save The Queen’ t-shirts quoted Sex Pistols lyrics to represent London punk culture during Queen Elizabeth’s “Silver Jubilee” celebration. The look was imbued with irony and featured the queen’s face over lyrics like “there is no future in England’s dream.”
Does the design of Selkie’s collection inspire rebellion? For many viewers, it appropriates antebellum style without necessarily calling it to question. It also brings to mind Elena Velez’s controversial ‘Gone With the Wind’ AW24 show which had many of the same underpinnings, but with an alt-right twist. Because of this misstep, fans are beginning to view the entire Selkie image in a new light. That is to say that they’re connecting violence with American nostalgism in ways that Selkie, a purveyor of 18th-19th century prairie dresses, formerly circumvented.
Can Selkie reclaim the flag for the girls and the gays?

posts.caption:
Wait, I was about to type “generate visuals if Ex Machina was set in Old Delhi” into my ChatGuPTa, but VegNonVeg had already sent me these 🕹️💅😌
A collaboration between the OG Indian sneaker concept store and the brand that made audio-tech f*cking cool. VNV turns 10, marking a decade of giving Indian sneakerheads (you know who you are) a dedicated space at the dawn of hypebeast culture. With their new Beats collaboration, they’ve tapped into a category I’d call editorial headphones.
Because let’s be honest, it has never been just about sound quality. It matters how headphones look on a person. By integrating Indian silver jewellery, a craft tradition with its own sonic history, they’ve created the accessory for the tech generation.
Everyone wish VegNonVeg a happy burrrdayyy! 🎂

posts.caption:
S is for Sarasa Textiles.
The label was founded by designer Maribel George after she found herself “unable to let go” of the work she was doing as part of the Venkatagiri Revival Project. They understand that one cannot claim to champion craft remotely from plush air-conditioned offices in tier-one cities, and has instead chosen to root itself in Venkatagiri itself. Here, they study the environment in which the cotton is woven, the women who wear it, and the myriad ways it is draped.
In a commercial landscape, where brands move from one emotional-support craft to another within a matter of months, Sarasa’s collections are developed over a span of up to four years, shaped by rigorous experimentation, historical inquiry, and deep textile study. Think Kanchipuram, Madras checks, and ikat enriched with exquisite zari, all woven in the quiet luxury (complimentary) of non-starched cotton, with saris ranging between ₹12,000 and ₹40,000.
Their campaigns, much like their saris, are strikingly contemporary yet steeped in nostalgia. They evoke the feeling of returning home. Like leafing through an old family photo album and finding elegant women in beautiful saris nestled between rain-soaked landscapes and images of lovingly prepared meals.
Sarasa might be making the most exquisite cotton saris, but what’s stands more brilliant is the complete abandonment of the fast modern fashion supply chain and still being able to be much more desirable than most bigger brands the market has to offer.

posts.caption:
Waiiittt! Is the printed sari back in conversation? Because right now stylists are not giving a second glance to a garment if it does not come with an endangered tradition tag. But Kangu walking out in this printed number for promotions, with 60s hairdo and rows of pearls to clutch and Miss Passi saying “hold my Leiber”, and pulling out a throwback in the same lewk (hair, pearls and all)? Much needed shade in this taapti dhoop lmaoooooo
But remember the 2010s?? When the coolest bunch of the millennial girlies brought the graphic printed sari to the mainstream at the dawn of Instagram. When it was all about statement prints and people went nuts with their Adobe softwares. If you could think it you probably printed it. And sari was not left behind. 9 yards of silk (or cheeky polyester lol) emblazoned with flowers, lipsticks, calligraphy, confectionaries and even Mickey Mouse (the Satya Paul and Disney collab anyone?). Anything that was under the sun could be on the sari. Probably the most expressive the garment ever was (?) DISCUSS ☕️👀💅🫦

posts.caption:
Presenting our first of four Summer Issue cover stars, @annehathaway.
Due to filming and release delays, Hathaway now has five movies premiering within six months. Her insanely busy year continues with her starring role as Penelope in @theodysseymovie. (To which she says: “It’s crazy to have four years of work coming out in six months.”) After weathering the storms of Hollywood, the in-demand star is finally collecting her flowers—and stopping to smell them. “If I’m not enjoying it, then really, really what’s wrong with me?” she tells ELLE. “If I can’t have a positive outlook on it when I’m having such a fortunate ride, then it would be wasted on me.”
For ELLE’s Summer 2026 issue, Hathaway reflects on how she’s grown since the early days of her career, reuniting with Christopher Nolan after 12 years, and navigating online criticism: “We’re at a time when people feel very confident in assuming what they think is fact.” Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Happy @oliviarodrigo album day to all who celebrate!
The singer’s new LP, ‘You Seem Pretty Sad for a Girl So in Love,’ has finally arrived, and it’s a wide-ranging account of a relationship that begins with a Pisces falling in love with a Gemini and ultimately ends with her processing the heartbreak after they break up. As she wrote herself in a note to fans upon its release: “The record is a time capsule of a relationship in all of its highs and lows. It’s my attempt at capturing love from both sides of the coin. The hope and disappointment. The insanity and the clarity. The entanglement and the unraveling.”
Rodrigo’s last public boyfriend was British actor Louis Partridge, whom she dated for two years—and ELLE is on the lyrical case, reading between the lines and connecting the dots with each re-listen. Swipe through for some songwriting standouts, and head to the link in bio for our investigation into some of the album’s meatiest tracks.

#ELLEDigitalCoverStar: There is a version of Kirandeep Chahal’s (@kirandeep_chahal) backstory that will hit close to home for many Indian women. To come from a family that cannot comprehend the desire for a life earned and lived on your own terms. To be expected to get married young, start a family, and follow a pre-written narrative folded neatly into the same shape it has always taken. Then there is the version she followed instead, one that eventually led her to becoming a familiar fixture on the runways of Dior, Dries Van Noten and Rick Owens, among other names that make fashion disciples starstruck.

posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: There is a version of Zinnia Kumar (now Rose) (@zinniakumar) that fashion understands easily. The model. An image that moves seamlessly between Paris, New York, and wherever the industry gathers next.
It is also the least interesting version of her.
We’re at Le Bristol in Paris and the day carries all the familiar markers of a luxury cover shoot. Controlled light. Precise framing. Cartier anchoring the narrative. But Rose doesn’t perform that script in the expected way. She arrives ready, slips into each look without negotiation, barely checks the monitor. There is no sense of overworking the image, only an instinctive understanding of how to move within it. Head to the 🔗 in bio to read our conversation with the cover star.

posts.caption:
#ELLEKulture: Thirteen years ago, seven young men from South Korea debuted with a dream. Today, BTS are not only one of the biggest groups in the world, but a cultural phenomenon that reshaped the music industry forever.
From 'No More Dream' to the record-shattering success of Arirang, BTS have broken barriers, topped charts, sold out stadiums, advocated for youth and mental health, and paved the way for greater Asian representation on the global stage.
In an industry where trends come and go, BTS have remained a force for nearly 13 years, proving that authenticity, artistry, and connection can transcend language, borders, and generations.
Swipe through to revisit the milestones that made BTS history makers. 💜
Words by: @16ektasinha

posts.caption:
#ELLEDigitalCoverStar: There is little illusion in the way Kirandeep Chahal (@kirandeep_chahal) speaks about modelling. Despite all its mythology, she understands the industry’s shelf life better than most people entering it. “In India, modelling still isn’t fully treated like a legitimate profession,” she says. Chahal admits there were multiple moments throughout her career where she nearly walked away from modelling entirely after difficult experiences on set or backstage. “There have been many times when I have done a show, and certain situations have happened where I’ve gone back and considered never modelling again,” she admits. How would she overcome it? “Never filter your thoughts,” she continues. “I would tell everybody coming up: always tell your truth.” Head to the 🔗 in bio to read the cover story.

When asked whether she ever thinks about the newer archetype of the “global Indian model”, the ambassador, the face, the culturally exportable version of beauty that luxury brands increasingly gravitate towards, she shrugs it off almost immediately. “You shouldn’t be the face of something despite of who you are,” she says. “You should be the face of something because of who you are.”

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by Shakaila Forbes-Bell on March 28, 2023 tagging @fumithelabel.

posts.caption:
The red hair and eyebrows have sparked a lot of conversation, but here’s the truth:
I didn’t do it to look younger.
I didn’t do it because it was trending.
I didn’t do it because it was flattering.
I did it because it felt fun.
And that’s something many of us don’t prioritise enough as adults.
A growing trend known as “whimsymaxxing” encourages people to intentionally seek out joy, novelty and playfulness in everyday life. While the term is new, the psychology behind it isn’t.
Research published in the Journal of Consumer Affairs found that cute and whimsical elements can generate feelings of healing and improve subjective wellbeing. The researchers suggest that these elements capture our attention and provide a temporary escape from stress, helping us feel restored.
In a world that constantly asks us to optimise, monetise and justify every decision, there is something deeply refreshing about doing something simply because it delights you.
So this is your reminder that not every style choice needs a strategic purpose.
Sometimes a red eyebrow is just a red eyebrow.
And sometimes that’s enough.
What’s your version of whimsymaxxing? 👇
Reference: Chou, H. Y., Chu, X. Y., & Chen, T. C. (2022). The healing effect of cute elements. Journal of Consumer Affairs, 56(2), 565-596.
Hair: @hair_evolve_by_de
#FashionPsychology #Whimsymaxxing #redhair

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠
posts.alt_text: Photo by Good On You: sustainability ratings on January 21, 2026.

posts.caption:
Meet the top rated brands of 2025 🎊⁠
⁠
In the past year, we rated more than 1500 brands and are proud to share the ten that received our highest marks, illustrating a commitment to more sustainable practices that protect people, the planet, and animals 💚⁠
⁠
What’s a theme that unites these brands? 👀 Outside of a commitment to reducing their impact, these brands are all small and independent, a reminder of where to look in the industry for leadership and innovation. If you’re looking for a reminder to support small brands this year, this is it.⁠
⁠
Cheers to all these brands, along with all the brands that were rated “Good” and “Great” in the past year! Here’s to continuing to make a difference and for championing a better approach to what we wear. Find the full list at the link—and share which brands you’re hoping to see rated in 2026 in the comments 💬⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
What can be accomplished in ten years?⁠
⁠
A decade may seem short but, in reflecting on all the work we’ve done in the past decade, we’re in awe of everything that has transpired—and are grateful for the hard work of the current and former team members along with you.⁠
⁠
Whether you’ve shared our posts or have been a partner in this business, the work of creating a more sustainable future isn’t done alone but in partnership with others. These accomplishments are an example of what happens when likeminded people come together in the hopes of a better world 🌍🌎🌏⁠

posts.caption:
How much should a t-shirt *actually* cost? 👕🤔⁠
⁠
When considering sustainability, there are many factors that go into understanding the “true” cost of a shit. Does this cost reflect workers being paid a living wage? Does this cost reflect ethically sourced recycled materials? Does this cost reflect the proper management of materials, as to not harm local environments? These are just a few of the questions to consider in understanding “how much” something like a t-shirt costs.⁠
⁠
In a new story for @guardian, our co-founder Gordon Renouf spoke with journalist Hilary Osborne to understand how to approach such questions. “Our ratings show that price is not a reliable indicator of ethical production,” Gordon says. “Many luxury and premium brands charge significantly more without demonstrating evidence of that value flowing to workers or environmental practices.” This is why “expensive” definitely does not correlate with “more ethical” ⚠️⁠
⁠
Gordon notes that many more ethical shirts are very, very affordable: he recommends brands like @rapanuiclothing, @notbasics, and @thtcclothing, among others, all of whom earn our highest ratings of “Good” and “Great”. Sustainability comes at a cost and, in many ways, it doesn’t cost *that* much, which is a great reminder that both luxury and fast fashion represent two ends of the market—and both are notorious for their lack of ethics.⁠
⁠
What do you think about the price of more sustainable clothing? Share in the comments, then read the full story at the link ↑ ⁠
⁠
#Shopping #TShirt #SustainableFashion #Basics #Sustainability⁠
posts.alt_text: Photo by Good On You: sustainability ratings on June 10, 2026. May be an image of card, poster, magazine, signboard and text that says '"A higher price is no guarantee, but a very low price is a strong warning sign." -Gordon Renouf, co-founder of Good On You What' the true cost of t-shirt? Ina a new story for The Guardian, our co-founder shares practical advice for making more ethical choices, while pointing out how an item' price doesn't always correlate with good values. Read more in the caption'.

posts.caption:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Matt Damon as Odysseus.
A Hollywood veteran of nearly five decades now, Damon has worked on approximately 80 film sets. And yet, as he told GQ’s @zachbaron, “Every single location on this movie would’ve been the hardest location on any other movie I’ve ever done… Every time we’d go somewhere, we’d be like, ‘Well, Iceland will be easier.’ And then it’s raining sideways and it’s fucking freezing. Iceland was like, ‘Yeah, easy? Hey, hold my beer.’”
For Damon, though, the challenge was part of what made the project so special: “All of the things that might have been difficult for me at an earlier point in my life weren’t difficult. They became fun. Like being wet and cold was not a hardship. It was actually something I felt really lucky to be experiencing. It felt temporary and like a gift.”
Damon unwraps the ‘gift’ that was making The Odyssey, alongside his co-stars Robert Pattinson and Tom Holland, and director Christopher Nolan, for GQ’s summer issue cover story at the 🔗 in bio.
Story by @zachbaron
Photographs by @alexprager

posts.caption:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Robert Pattinson as Antinous.
Pattinson’s part in The Odyssey is not big—he plays the cockiest of the many suitors who have installed themselves at Ithaca, seeking the hand of Penelope (played by Anne Hathaway) while Matt Damon’s Odysseus is away. “My character’s a little like James Woods in Casino. That was my inspiration for it,” Pattinson said. “He’s like a little sleazy. I kept saying at my costume fitting, ‘I really want to have leopard underpants.’”
Pattinson talks to GQ’s @zachbaron about teaming up with Nolan again for The Odyssey, alongside co-stars Matt Damon and Tom Holland, at the 🔗 in bio.

posts.caption:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Tom Holland as Telemachus.
When Christopher Nolan first offered Holland a part in The Odyssey, the production dates were identical to those of Spider-Man: Brand New Day (and Holland’s commitments to the Spider-Man franchise are ironclad). “I said to Chris, like, ‘Look, I want to do this movie, but if I’m going to do it, I’m going to have to call Sony and have a very uncomfortable conversation,’ ” Holland said. Then he immediately had a very uncomfortable conversation.
“Chris has that reputation of ‘This movie isn’t going to go five months over, and we aren’t actually going to lose Tom for two years,’ ” Holland said. “Any other director, it might have been a slightly different conversation.” The Odyssey started on the day Nolan said it would and finished nine days early.
For our summer cover story, Holland talks about coming of age on set, getting to star alongside his partner on another project, and how he’s approaching the future of his career at the 🔗 in bio.

posts.caption: For David Hockney, style was never just about appearances—it was a way of living in and seeing the world. Today, we are honoring the wildly influential British artist, who died Thursday at 88, that moved across a wide range of disciplines, from painting and drawing to photography, opera and ballet design, film, and works made on iPhones and iPads. A proud Yorkshireman and longtime Angeleno, he captured both the English countryside and California’s sun-drenched vistas while helping redefine figurative art for a new era. Just last year, the Foundation Louis Vuitton in Paris mounted a landmark retrospective of Hockney’s vast output, bringing together more than 400 works spanning seven decades of innovation. He placed queer love, intimacy, and life at the center of his work, helping broaden who could be seen—and celebrated—in contemporary art. In a 2011 interview with Harper’s Bazaar, he reflected on his enduring creative momentum. Click the link in our bio to revisit the story and his iconic career.

posts.caption: Making paintings is what @mickalenethomas does best. Her process involves staging photo shoots, directing models through highly stylized scenes. Then, the photographs are cut and collaged, and from these, she builds an idea of the image that will then be translated onto a painted canvas. The paintings—portraits of Black women, often inspired by her late mother, Sandra Bush—are then finished with glitter, sequins, appliquéd fabrics, or rhinestones, among other materials. At the link in our bio, the multi-hyphenate speaks with Bazaar's @pleaseemailmax about her takes on Black masculinity and what to expect from her show opening at the Shephard as part of the “First, Now, Next” Questionnaire.

posts.caption: Coming off Fall 2026 fashion month this past March, we were left with a lingering, wistful feeling of romance. It seemed that same idea was simultaneously mimicked across film, television, literature, and music in cases like Sarah J. Mass's hugely popular "ACOTAR" series, the 2026 "Wuthering Heights" film, even @OliviaRodrigo's recent "Drop Dead" music video. Romance presents itself in so many different forms, only some of which seem to have anything to do with the fashion’s interpretation of the concept. So we wonder, does today’s romantic aesthetic have anything to do with romance? Tap the link in bio for all of the details.

posts.caption:
Spanish knitwear label @babaa has grown from founder Marta Bahillo’s living room project into a beloved Madrid-based brand built on local production and long-term artisan relationships. What began in 2012 as children’s sweaters now spans womenswear, childrenswear, and a small but growing menswear line.⁠
⁠
Speaking to us from Madrid, Bahillo reflects on keeping production in Spain, working closely with her network of makers, and gradually translating babaà’s classic pieces for men. “You're investing in these relationships; it's not just transactional,” she says. “Good work never comes from just one encounter.”

posts.caption:
When @tagheuer debuted the Monaco in 1969, its unconventional square dial was considered avant-garde. While many watch purists initially dismissed the design, Steve McQueen famously wore the Monaco in Le Mans (1971), helping transform it into one of the most iconic race watches ever made.⁠
⁠
Now, TAG Heuer is pushing the Monaco into radical new territory with the Monaco Speed 12, a 50-piece limited edition priced at $87,000. Unveiled at the Formula 1 Louis Vuitton Grand Prix de Monaco 2026, the watch replaces the traditional dial with 12 numbered pistons inspired by a V12 engine, powered by Louis Vuitton’s Spin Time movement developed by La Fabrique du Temps. Encased in Grade 5 titanium, the Monaco Speed 12 looks like a high-performance engine trapped inside a square glass frame, delivering one of the boldest interpretations of the Monaco to date.⁠
posts.alt_text: Photo by Highsnobiety Style #HSStyle on June 12, 2026. May be an image of sweater, fleece, knit, cardigan and text that says 'IT TAKES A VILLAGE TO ΜΑK THESE KNITS'.

posts.caption:
What happens when one of Berlin's most influential cultural platforms collaborates with one of its most forward-thinking automotive brands?⁠
⁠
For their latest partnership, @cupra_de and @032c unveiled the CUPRA Raval through an immersive installation inspired by the city's visual energy, accompanied by a capsule of accessories that reimagined everyday automotive objects through the lens of design, utility, and self-expression.⁠
⁠
From an oversized inflatable car cover to bomber-inspired safety vests and customized charging accessories, the collaboration explored the intersection of mobility, fashion, and contemporary culture.⁠
⁠
Tap the link in bio for the full story.⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
The 2026 World Cup is finally here, but we’re still hung up on what happened 20 years ago. We’re not talking about Italy’s fourth trophy or Zidane’s head-butt incident. No, our eyes were fixed solely on the stands, @victoriabeckham and every Hérmes Birkin she brought to Germany that summer. 2006 was the golden era of WAGs, and we’ll probably never see anything like it again. Everybody say thank you Victoria, Carly, Coleen, Abbey, Toni and Cheryl. ’06 is still on our World Cup fashion moodboard, and we’re bringing out the layered tank tops this summer.
Images: Getty

posts.caption:
@limifeu_official planted an immersive installation at Yohji Yamamoto’s Wooster Street space. 🎟️
As part of her residency with her father’s label, the installation is a full-store takeover, anchored by an exclusive capsule with artist Eri Wakiyama (@mermaidhair ).
The collection spans four tees and a statement shirt, alongside a collectible doll and keychain handmade by the artist herself. A collection of Wakiyama’s illustrations has also hit the Yohji store, and to celebrate the launch, we took a closer look at the art that inspired it all.
The installation is now on view at 52 Wooster until June 14.

posts.caption:
There are people who wear time, and then there are people who define it. @palaktiwarii belongs to the second kind.✨
She carries something rare, a weight that isn’t borrowed from the past, but isn’t chasing the future either. It simply exists, with the kind of quiet confidence that doesn’t need to announce itself.
In this frame, legacy and the now collapse into each other. @guesswatches on her wrist, intent in her eyes, highlighting the “Main Character” hunger behind the gold hardware; this is a story of a woman who isn’t just wearing the accessories of success, but is actively forging them. ⌚️
The Architecture of Time isn’t a concept. It’s a posture. A way of owning every second without letting a single one own you.
@palaktiwarii doesn’t follow the tempo. She sets it. And right now, she’s setting it for all of us. 🫶🏻

posts.caption:
@jennifer.garner *always* knows what to do (and it usually involves sorghum).
Between starring in summer’s buzziest new series, taking her near-billion dollar company public, and turning ‘Pretend Cooking Show’ into a reality, she still manages to be the nicest woman in Hollywood.
Playing a widowed food blogger in @Peacock’s adaptation of @elinhilderbrand’s bestseller ‘The Five-Star Weekend,’ the beloved actress and entrepreneur channels her inner Ina Garten for a not-so-Instagrammable girls’ weekend on Nantucket with co-stars-turned-IRL besties, @chloesevigny, @morereginahall, @gemmachan and @darcycarden, exploring what it means to grow and develop in middle age.
At 54, the Marvel Hero and supermom reflects on her genre-spanning career, being “total besties” with John Cena, her surprisingly vast knowledge of weaponry, plus the responsibility of celebrities to stand up for working locally for InStyle’s Summer Imagemakers Issue at the link in @instylemagazine’s bio now.

Meet some of the tastemakers who believe Pride is a year-round philosophy that shapes everything they create at the link in bio.

A different sound that the country still isn’t used to, an identity that’s proclaimed proudly, and an artist who believes that the real thing doesn’t need too much explaining. Anoushka Maskey is doing things on her terms - she tells us in an exclusive interview.

posts.caption:
Yes, it’s L’OFFICIEL! Cosa hanno in comune “Dune: Part Two”, “Furiosa: A Mad Max Saga” e la nuova serie Apple TV “Lucky”? Sono tutti ambientati nel deserto—e tutti vedono protagonista @AnyaTaylorJoy. «Mi diverte sempre, perché non capisco cosa ci sia nei miei capelli, nel mio incarnato o nella larghezza dei miei occhi che mi renda adatta a interpretare una creatura del deserto. Eppure la gente adora mettermi lì», confessa Taylor-Joy a L’OFFICIEL. «Avverto una vera affinità con questi mondi».
Dal pianeta brullo di Arrakis in “Dune” al Wasteland che domina “Furiosa: A Mad Max Saga”, la nostra protagonista di giugno continua a ritrovarsi negli scenari più ostili del cinema contemporaneo. «Il deserto possiede una sacralità propria», osserva. Alla domanda su cosa ami di questi spazi desolati, la risposta è immediata: «L’alba e il tramonto, soprattutto. Perché vedi i colori più straordinari e senti di essere l’unica persona sulla terra».

posts.caption:
Introducing our global cover star, @anyataylorjoy.
“I’ve always been nomadic by nature,” the British-Argentine actor tells L’OFFICIEL. “Now I’ve just fully embraced it as part of who I am. If I have a couple of days off, I’m always like, ‘Ooh, what’s within driving distance?’ If it’s six hours or less, I’m like, ‘Let’s go. Why not?’”
Fresh off of a recent desert road trip, Taylor-Joy—who has cemented her status as a fashion darling through ambassadorships with @Dior and @TiffanyandCo—is preparing for the release of Apple TV’s ‘Lucky,’ a thriller about a con artist caught in the fallout of a multimillion-dollar heist gone wrong. The role marks another chapter in a career spanning ‘The Witch,’ ‘The Queen’s Gambit,’ ‘Furiosa,’ and ‘Dune.’
In ‘Lucky,’ her character is pursued by the FBI and a ruthless mob boss while searching for something more elusive than freedom: stability. “I was so attracted to playing somebody who was just so desperate to stay still and put down roots,” says Taylor-Joy.
Filmed across the desert and inside Las Vegas’s Caesars Palace, the production left its mark. “I have a real soft spot for Caesars Palace now—we’ve been through a lot together,” she laughs.
At the link in bio, read our full conversation with Taylor-Joy on inhabiting new characters, life on the road, and ‘Lucky.’

posts.caption:
#DavidHockney è morto a 88 anni. Capelli biondo ossigenato e occhiali tondi, è stato tra i protagonisti assoluti dell'arte del secondo Novecento. Dalle piscine californiane di “A Bigger Splash” ai ritratti dell'intimità gay dipinti quando l'omosessualità in Gran Bretagna era ancora reato, fino ai disegni su iPad e al fregio di 91 metri dipinto durante il lockdown, non smise mai di lavorare. Nel 2018 “Portrait of an Artist (Pool with Two Figures)” fu battuto all'asta per 90 milioni di dollari, record per un artista vivente. Pensava che ridere ogni giorno facesse vivere per sempre, e nei sette decenni di lavoro ha seguito un'unica regola: dipingere le cose che amava. Leggi l'articolo a cura di @AlessandroViapiana_ al link in bio.
Photo:
1, 4 e 6. Getty Images
2. David Hockney, Portrait of an Artist (Pool with Two Figures), 1972 Acrylic on canvas 213.36 x 304.8 cm (84 x 120 Inches) © David Hockney | Photo Credit: Art Gallery of New South Wales / Jenni Carter (Courtesy of Fondation Louis Vuitton)
3. Courtesy of Fondation Louis Vuitton
5. David Hockney, A Bigger Splash, 1967; Acrylic on canvas 242.5 x 243.9 x 3 cm (96 x 96 x 1.181 Inches) © David Hockney Tate, U.K. (Courtesy of Fondation Louis Vuitton)
#Art #PopArt

posts.caption:
Dall’intuizione alla forma, dalla materia alla luce. @DamianiOfficial presenta Arte Maestra — una collezione di Alta Gioielleria nata dallo studio di otto capolavori della storia dell’arte: da Caravaggio a Botticelli, da Hokusai a Klimt, da Monet a Van Gogh. Ogni gioiello è un’interpretazione, ogni pietra un dettaglio plasmato per raggiungere l’equilibrio perfetto tra luce, forma e movimento. Per il lancio, un cast d’eccezione: @ChiaraFerragni, @MariacarlaBoscono, @ElenaSofiaRicci_OfficialPage, @AchilleIdol. Scopri di più al link in bio.

posts.caption:
Introducing our global cover star, @anyataylorjoy.
“I’ve always been nomadic by nature,” the British-Argentine actor tells L’OFFICIEL. “Now I’ve just fully embraced it as part of who I am. If I have a couple of days off, I’m always like, ‘Ooh, what’s within driving distance?’ If it’s six hours or less, I’m like, ‘Let’s go. Why not?’”
Fresh off of a recent desert road trip, Taylor-Joy—who has cemented her status as a fashion darling through ambassadorships with @Dior and @TiffanyandCo—is preparing for the release of Apple TV’s ‘Lucky,’ a thriller about a con artist caught in the fallout of a multimillion-dollar heist gone wrong. The role marks another chapter in a career spanning ‘The Witch,’ ‘The Queen’s Gambit,’ ‘Furiosa,’ and ‘Dune.’
In ‘Lucky,’ her character is pursued by the FBI and a ruthless mob boss while searching for something more elusive than freedom: stability. “I was so attracted to playing somebody who was just so desperate to stay still and put down roots,” says Taylor-Joy.
Filmed across the desert and inside Las Vegas’s Caesars Palace, the production left its mark. “I have a real soft spot for Caesars Palace now—we’ve been through a lot together,” she laughs.
At the link in bio, read our full conversation with Taylor-Joy on inhabiting new characters, life on the road, and ‘Lucky.’

posts.caption:
Welcome to the June issue, where L’OFFICIEL explores travel not as movement, but as a state of mind. Our “nomadic by nature” cover star @AnyaTaylorJoy discusses her love of road trips, filming the new Apple TV series ‘Lucky’ in the Las Vegas desert, and her unique approach to learning her characters. The enigmatic photographer @SzilveszterMako, renowned for his surreal images, cinematic backdrops, and dreamlike scenes, turned his lens on the actress, capturing her shifting world for our shoot.
Elsewhere, the issue focuses on slow travel and the art of pausing. We take you to Grand Cayman, Ischia, and Costa Rica, where hydrotherapy and relaxation define the experience, and across the Canadian Rockies, Arabian Desert, and Australian Outback by rail—the defining way to travel in 2026. Elsewhere, we follow one writer into the next frontier of luxury wellness: sleep optimization.
In Paris, we sit down with Hong Kong–born couturier @RobertWun, who has made a name for himself with dramatic, over-the-top silhouettes; he reminds us that slowing down is sometimes the most radical imaginative act. And in Tbilisi, it’s the creative class who are putting the Georgian capital on the map—and we have your guide to everything that’s happening now.
On the fashion front, Spring/Summer 2026’s finest looks head to Cape Town for a sun-drenched escape, while long-heralded summer heroes—unexpected denim and easy dresses—capture the spirit of going nowhere in good company, this time in Los Angeles’s canyons. Stay tuned for our issue to hit newsstands this month.

posts.caption:
#DavidHockney, one of the most influential artists of the last century, has died at 88.
Born in Bradford, England, Hockney rose to prominence in the 1960s and became a leading figure in the Pop Art movement. But unlike many of his contemporaries, who focused on consumer culture and commercialism, Hockney found inspiration in the people, places, and moments around him.
After moving to Los Angeles, he became famous for his vibrant portraits and sun-drenched pool paintings, creating a style that would become instantly recognizable around the world. Over a career spanning more than six decades, Hockney helped redefine contemporary art with his bold use of color, light, and perspective. His work leaves behind a legacy that shaped generations of artists and art lovers alike.
Photos Getty Images
#davidhockneyart

posts.caption:
Ahead of the 2026 FIFA World Cup, New York City gets a citywide art takeover.
The city is shifting into celebration mode, with public spaces across the boroughs and the wider region coming alive through sport, culture, and design. Commissioned by @arts14c in partnership with the FIFA World Cup 2026 New York New Jersey Host Committee, ‘The Art of the Game’ brings together established and emerging artists connected to institutions like @themuseumofmodernart, @metmuseum, and the @whitneymuseum. Each artist reimagines the iconic soccer ball as a large-scale public artwork, fabricated in Brooklyn and assembled in Jersey City, grounding a global sporting moment in the region’s local creative landscape. Read more on the art takeover at the link in our bio.
Photos courtesy of ARTS 14C.
#fifaworldcup #fifaworldcup2026

posts.caption:
As our June issue is dedicated to travel, we’ve mapped out an itinerary of our own—one centered on well-deserved rest and fun in the sun. Our first dreamy destination? Cape Town, South Africa.⁠
⁠
From the city’s sandy beaches to its vibrant streets, the standout styles of the season capture the art of dressing with intention while maintaining an effortless ease. Fashion’s warm-weather mood is far from over, and before the industry fully embraces the buttoned-up allure of fall, we’re lingering a little longer in the glow of pre-fall.⁠
⁠
From breezy @Dior dresses and @Chanelofficial’s lightweight tweeds to the dazzling heels born from @Balenciaga and @ManoloBlahnik’s recent collaboration, these are the pieces defining warm-weather dressing right now. So sit back, relax, and follow along—L’OFFICIEL’s curated itinerary is one you won’t want to miss. Read more at the link in our bio. ⁠

posts.caption:
@Balenciaga dropped its Spring Summer 2026 preview collection in Mumbai last night, and the energy in the room felt different.
With Fatima Sana Sheikh, Bobby Deol, Mouni Roy, Sanya Malhotra, Malavika Mohanan, and Krithi Shetty in attendance, it felt less like a formal showcase and more like a real moment around fashion. The room was sharp, observant, and engaged.
Fatima Sana Shaikh said it best: “Balenciaga always has a chic approach and the brand is now heading towards it even more aggressively.” And that tone carried through the evening. Nothing felt loud for attention, yet everything commanded it.
What stood out was how people responded. There was curiosity, closer inspection, and genuine interest in the details. Some silhouettes felt familiar but more refined, while others brought a quiet sense of drama without trying too hard.
That is when a collection works.
Not when it demands attention, but when it earns it.
@balenciaga #Balenciaga

posts.caption:
Jacob & Co. continues to blur the line between watchmaking, sculpture, and fantasy with the Astronomia “Gates of Olympus.”
Priced at $1.4 million, the one-of-one creation draws inspiration from Greek mythology, featuring Zeus, Olympus, celestial movement, and a fully mechanical miniature world built directly into the timepiece itself.
Inside the 18K rose gold case sits a triple-axis tourbillon, over 9 carats of baguette diamonds, and an intricate Olympus scene designed as part of the movement.
More than a watch, the piece reflects how modern haute horology is increasingly moving toward collectible art and immersive storytelling.
@JacobAndCo #JacobAndCo #Luxurious #Collab

posts.caption:
Buying a Bugatti is only the beginning.
While a Chiron can cost around $3 million, ownership comes with expenses few cars in the world can match. A set of specially engineered tires can cost around $40,000, annual maintenance can exceed $25,000, and even a routine oil change can run into five figures. Add insurance, carbon-ceramic brakes, specialized servicing, and climate-controlled storage, and the cost of ownership quickly climbs far beyond the purchase price.
For most luxury cars, buying the vehicle is the biggest expense. For a Bugatti, keeping it running is often the real luxury.
#Luxurious #Bugatti
